message.content:
Adversaries may exploit Unix shell commands and scripts for execution on Linux and macOS systems, leveraging various shell types (e.g., sh, bash, zsh). Unix shells can control system aspects, with some commands requiring elevated privileges. Shell scripts support sequential command execution and typical programming operations, making them useful for repetitive tasks or running commands across multiple systems. Adversaries use Unix shells to execute commands or payloads, often through command and control channels or during lateral movement (e.g., SSH). Shell scripts can deliver and execute multiple commands, aiding in persistence and payload deployment.

Common exploitation methods include creating reverse shells, executing arbitrary commands, and leveraging shell scripts for persistence and payload execution. Notable adversaries and malware using this technique include AppleJeus, APT41, Bundlore, CallMe, Chaos, CoinTicker, CookieMiner, Derusbi, Doki, Drovorub, Exaramel for Linux, Fysbis, Green Lambert, Hildegard, Kazuar, Kinsing, Kobalos, LoudMiner, MacMa, NETWIRE, OSX/Shlayer, OSX_OCEANLOTUS.D, Penquin, Proton, Rocke, Skidmap, TeamTNT, Turian, WindTail, and XCSSET.

Mitigation strategies include restricting shell access, monitoring and logging shell activity, employing principle of least privilege, using application whitelisting, and implementing robust endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions to detect and block malicious shell activity.
